text.title: React Hooks Rules
text.summary: Concrete rules for correct hook usage: call order, dependencies, cleanup, and memoization.

rule.statement: Call hooks only at the top level of a function component or custom hook. Never inside if, loops, nested functions, or after an early return.
rule.rationale: React relies on call order to associate state with the correct hook slot. Conditional calls change the order between renders, corrupting fiber state in ways that produce silent stale values or outright crashes.

rule.statement: Call hooks only from React function components or custom hooks. Never from plain functions, class methods, or event handlers.
rule.rationale: Hooks use React's internal dispatcher, which is only active inside a component render. Calling outside it throws a runtime error in development and corrupts state in production.

rule.statement: List every value used inside useEffect, useCallback, or useMemo in the dependency array. Never silence the react-hooks/exhaustive-deps ESLint rule with a comment.
rule.rationale: Missing dependencies create stale closures: the effect captures an old value on mount and never re-runs when it changes. This produces bugs that only appear after state transitions and are extremely hard to reproduce.

rule.statement: When a dep is genuinely unwanted (stable callback reference), refactor: extract the value to a ref, use useReducer to eliminate the dependency, or use the useEvent pattern — do not suppress the lint rule.
rule.rationale: The lint rule exists precisely because every suppressed warning is a future stale-closure bug. Refactoring eliminates the dependency legitimately; suppressing the warning only hides it.

rule.statement: Return a cleanup function from every useEffect that subscribes, starts a timer, or opens a connection.
rule.rationale: React Strict Mode mounts, unmounts, and remounts components in development to surface missing cleanup. A missing cleanup causes memory leaks, duplicate subscriptions, and state updates on unmounted components.

rule.statement: For async data fetching inside useEffect, create an AbortController, pass signal to fetch, and call abort() in the cleanup.
rule.rationale: Without abort, the response from a previous URL arrives after a URL change, setting stale data. AbortController prevents this race condition without introducing a boolean flag.

rule.statement: Prefer deriving values during render over syncing them via useState + useEffect. const fullName = first + " " + last replaces a synced fullName state.
rule.rationale: Synced state creates a second copy of truth that can drift. The useEffect fires after render, causing an extra render cycle. Derived values compute once per render with zero additional state.

rule.statement: Use useCallback only when passing a function to a memoized child (React.memo) or using it as a dependency of another hook.
rule.rationale: useCallback wrapping adds overhead — a new hook slot and a dependency check — with no benefit unless the reference stability is consumed. Measure before memoizing.

rule.statement: Use useMemo for computationally expensive operations (sorts, filters over large arrays). Not for object identity stability of trivial values.
rule.rationale: Trivial useMemo (const x = useMemo(() => a + b, [a, b])) costs more than the bare computation. React 19 compiler will handle most memoization automatically; write clear code first.
